screens.name: Song Score Detail
screens.purpose: Present dense lead-sheet chord symbols with established Jazz shorthand and perceptually clear chord grouping.

ux_rules: Preserve the approved Jazz shorthand mapping and baseline-versus-raised semantic hierarchy.
Judge readability by visible chord grouping and minimum whitespace, not by non-overlapping bounding boxes alone.
Prefer fewer measures per mobile system over shrinking chord symbols below a comfortable reading size.
Keep root, quality, unaltered extension, and slash bass on the primary baseline.
Keep raised alterations and modifiers subordinate but large enough to read without zooming.
Preserve unsupported source text visibly and do not mutate source song data.

screen_contexts.layout_intent: Use responsive system density and restrained chord-part scaling so every chord reads as a distinct musical object at 390px.
interaction_risks: Moving every chord to a centered anchor can improve one pair while worsening long slash-chord pairs.
Using SVG super directly raises modifiers too far and makes them look detached.
Keeping four measures per system can leave less than one pixel of visible whitespace even after shorthand compression.
notes: Refinement baseline: the densest approved shorthand pair has only 0.6px of visible whitespace.
The first refinement experiment centered every chord and enlarged modifiers to 78%; it was rejected because another long-code pair fell to 0.6px.
The current 70% modifier renders at 9.576px and rises 9px above the root, making it look like a detached footnote.
Use three to five browser-backed iterations as explicitly requested by the user.

constraints: Keep the approved △, −, °, ø, +, ♭, and ♯ mapping unchanged.
Do not globally center chord anchors; the first refinement experiment showed regressions for long slash chords.
Test three measures per row at widths up to 640px while retaining four per row on larger screens.
Increase raised modifier size from 70% into the 76-80% range.
Replace the browser-defined super position with an explicit, smaller rise tuned in the rendered score.
Target at least 4px visible whitespace between adjacent chord groups on representative dense rows when practical.
Do not reduce the baseline chord size below the current rendered 13.68px at 390px.
Capture and compare each iteration before accepting it.
Do not change source song strings, alphaTex generation, global theme variables, or dependencies.
open_decisions: Choose the smallest explicit modifier rise that preserves semantic hierarchy without appearing detached.
Choose between 76%, 78%, and 80% modifier scale based on the 390px screenshots.

critical_judgments: Can A♭△7 and G−7 be identified immediately as two separate chords?
Do long slash chords remain separate from their neighbors?
Does raised ♭9 or ♯9 read as part of its chord rather than as an annotation above it?
Does sus4 remain subordinate without becoming too small?
Does the additional system count remain a reasonable mobile scrolling tradeoff?
Are there no row, header, or page-width regressions at 390px?
must_not_regress: non-chord SVG text and staff engraving
stored chord strings and alphaTex generation

visual_constraints: Use three mobile measures per system if it materially improves chord grouping.
Keep baseline symbols at least the current mobile size.
Use 76-80% scale for raised parts and an explicit rise smaller than SVG super.
Aim for at least 4px visible separation between adjacent chord groups in representative dense systems.
The page must not create horizontal overflow.
Final evidence must include 기억의 습작 and a sharp-alteration sample at 390px.
